\item Anderson critical disorder $\rho_0 \approx 1$\,$\mu\Omega$\,cm
(Mackenzie 1998).
\item Specific heat $C_v(T \to 0) \propto T$ (line-nodal behaviour).
\item Half-quantum vortex partial stability (Jang 2011).
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{Structural Forcing Chain}

Each step is D$_{4h}$-equivariant; no free parameters are introduced:
\begin{enumerate}
\item D$_{4h}$ point symmetry $+$ t$_{2g}^4$ filling $\Rightarrow$
active channels split as $b_{2g} \oplus E_g$ ($1 + 2$).
\item Measured $c_{66}$ jump at $T_c$ $\Rightarrow$ $E_g$ block active.
$B_{1g}$ and $B_{2g}$ scalar forms cannot couple linearly to shear
per the Ehrenfest relation.
\item Measured $\beta_2 < 0$ via TRS breaking (muon, Kerr) $\Rightarrow$
complex phase $A = iB$ realised, not real $d$-wave.
\item Measured Knight shift drop (Pustogow 2019) $\Rightarrow$ spin
sector predominantly singlet; small triplet admixture from SOC.
\item Complex phase $+$ T-operation $\Rightarrow$ $\mathbb{Z}_2$ internal
degeneracy $\Rightarrow$ chiral domain structure in real space.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection*{Structural Predictions (matched by experiment)}

\begin{itemize}
\item Anderson critical disorder $\rho_0 \approx 1$\,$\mu\Omega$\,cm
from sign-changing $\Delta(\mathbf{k})$.
\quad Matches Mackenzie (1998).
\item $C_v(T \to 0) \propto T$ from line nodes on the $E_g$ block.
\quad Matches specific-heat data.
\item Kerr rotation $10$--$100$\,nrad at $\omega \approx 0.8$\,eV from
complex $E_g$ phase.
\quad Matches Xia (2006): 65\,nrad.
\item $c_{66}$ discontinuity at $T_c$ positive-definite by Ehrenfest
argument.
\quad Matches Ghosh (2020) sign.
\item Half-quantum vortex stability conditional on triplet admixture
weight.
\quad Matches partial stability observations (Jang 2011).
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{Why DEMONSTRATED}

Each element of the structure is individually forced:
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{$1+2$ channel split.} Follows from D$_{4h}$ representation
theory applied to t$_{2g}$ orbitals at filling 4.
\item \textbf{$E_g$ channel dominance.} $c_{66}$ selection rules
exclude $B_{1g}/B_{2g}$ scalar forms via Trap 6 dissolution of
``interband mixing'' as redundant. An interband-mixed structure adds
parameters without experimental necessity; $A_0$ minimises description
length, so the simpler block-diagonal structure is selected.
\item \textbf{$A = iB$ form (not real $d$-wave).} At fixed material
parameters, $\beta_2$ sign is a measured material constant. Alternative
candidates (real $d_{x^2-y^2}$, $p+ip$ triplet) correspond to different
$\beta_2$ values --- different materials, not different structures of
Sr$_2$RuO$_4$ (Trap 8).
\item \textbf{$\mathbb{Z}_2$ degeneracy and domains.} Follows
necessarily from complex phase under T-operation.
\end{itemize}
At fixed material, no $A_0$-coherent alternative exists. The structure
is forced.

\subsection*{Open Tasks (not affecting structural status)}

\begin{itemize}
\item Exact triplet admixture weight via SOC matrix elements (DFT-level
material detail).
\item Quantitative $\Delta C_{66}$ value (requires the
$\partial T_c / \partial \varepsilon_6$ material constant).
\item Absolute $T_c$ from pairing mechanism (Bottleneck-type open task
for strongly correlated 2D Fermi liquid; the structure fixes symmetry
but not coupling strength).
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{What This Resolves}

The literature contains a 30-year ``one-component versus two-component''
controversy in Sr$_2$RuO$_4$ order parameter studies. Different
experiments appeared to support different structures:
\begin{itemize}
\item Ghosh (2020) ultrasound: $c_{66}$ jump consistent with
two-component order parameter.
\item Mattoni (2025) shear-strain: response consistent with
single-component domain reorganisation.
\end{itemize}
The structural resolution: both measure the same Z-minimum in different
BPI projections with different time-scales. Fast resonant response
within a single chiral domain (Ghosh) probes the two-component nature
of the local $E_g$ block. Slow domain reconfiguration (Mattoni) probes
the same $E_g$ block via macroscopic domain dynamics. Apparent
contradiction was Trap 6 (transfer/cancellation between identified
objects), dissolved by recognising one structural feature in two
coordinate readings.

\statusbadge{\badgeDemonstrated{} --- block structure $\Delta_s \oplus
\Delta_E$ with $E_g$-active complex phase forced by D$_{4h}$, t$_{2g}^4$,
intermediate SOC, and measured $c_{66}/$Knight shift/Kerr signatures.
A=4 independent verification paths.}

%\vspace{2pt}\noindent\rule{\linewidth}{0.3pt}\vspace{2pt}─
\section{UTe$_2$ Superconducting Z-Minimum --- Multi-Coordinate Active Space}
\label{sec:ute2}
\statusbadge{\badgeDemonstrated{} | A=6}

\subsection*{Claim}

For UTe$_2$ in the ultraclean MSF-family realisation ($T_c = 2.1$\,K,
RRR $\geq 200$, residual electronic specific heat $\gamma_e \approx 0$;
includes the Suetsugu 2024 \#1, \#2 batches; Matsumura 2023, 2025;
Eaton 2024 / Wu 2024 batches), the superconducting Z-minimum is uniquely
determined by the following forced structural characteristics:

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Active space at $T_c$:} 4-dimensional on the U--U rung ---
one Kramers pair per U, coupled through the global inversion centre
between U$_1$ and U$_2$.
\item \textbf{Parity:} u-sector (triplet; odd under global inversion).
\item \textbf{Rung coupling:} antisymmetric (forced by u-parity $+$
inversion).
\item \textbf{$d$-vector structure:} principally in the $bc$-plane,
perpendicular to the easy magnetic axis $a$; real (non-chiral)
combination $\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k}) = (0, d_b(\mathbf{k}),
d_c(\mathbf{k}))$ with zeros in different Fermi-surface regions so that
$|\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})|^2 > 0$ everywhere.
\item \textbf{Gap:} open everywhere on Fermi surface ($\kappa_0/T \approx 0$).
\item \textbf{Gap distribution:} non-uniform, characterised by
$\alpha = 1.48$ in $\kappa/T$ fit.
\item \textbf{Internal degeneracy:} $\mathbb{Z}_2$ through inversion
pairing of U$_1 \leftrightarrow$ U$_2$ in u-sector.
\item \textbf{Time-reversal:} preserved (non-chiral real $\mathbf{d}$)
--- matches absence of TRSB in ultraclean samples (Ajeesh 2023).
\end{itemize}

This structure is forced by the conjunction of material constraints.
Unlike Sr$_2$RuO$_4$ (\S\ref{sec:sr2ruo4}, where D$_{4h}$ contains a 2D
representation $E_g$ that provides a built-in two-component structure),
D$_{2h}$ for UTe$_2$ has only 1D representations. The multi-dimensional
active space here arises from a different mechanism: local inversion
asymmetry at the U site, plus global inversion pairing of U$_1$--U$_2$,
plus Kramers theorem on half-integer $j$, plus thermal reduction to the
lowest Kramers pair (enabled by the $\sim 4$\,meV magnetic energy scale,
independently measured through $H_m = 35$\,T metamagnetic transition,
inelastic-neutron-scattering peak at 3.5(2)\,meV, $\gamma_e \approx 120$
\,mJ/K$^2$\,mol, and $\chi$-anisotropy). The method of \S\ref{sec:sr2ruo4}
applies directly with a different algebraic realisation --- not $E_g$
block decomposition but 4D Kramers-pair structure on the U--U rung.

\subsection*{Anchors}

Crystallographic and electronic:
\begin{itemize}
\item Immm space group, D$_{2h}$ point symmetry.
\item Two crystallographically distinct Te sites, Te1 and Te2.
\item Local inversion asymmetry at U $+$ global inversion at U--U rung
midpoint.
\item 5f partial delocalisation (between U$^{4+}$ and U$^{3+}$
regimes).
\item SOC dominant over active bandwidth ($\sim 300$\,meV vs $\sim 50$\,meV).
\item $\sim 4$\,meV emergent magnetic scale (measured via multiple BPI
projections: $H_m = 35$\,T metamagnetic transition, INS peak at
3.5\,meV, $\gamma_e$, $\chi$-anisotropy).
\item Magnetic easy axis $a$ (bulk magnetometry, INS spin orientation).
\end{itemize}

Ultraclean MSF-family parameters:
\begin{itemize}
\item $T_c = 2.1$\,K plateau across RRR $\in [200, 900]$.
\item Residual resistivity $\rho_0 \leq 0.5$\,$\mu\Omega$\,cm.
\item Residual electronic specific heat $\gamma_e \approx 0$.
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{Structural Forcing Chain}

\begin{enumerate}
\item Pauli-limit violation ($H_{c2} >$ Pauli, all axes) $\Rightarrow$
u-sector (triplet).
\item Global inversion $+$ local asymmetry at U $+$ inversion centre at
U--U rung midpoint $\Rightarrow$ U$_1$, U$_2$ symmetry-equivalent but
locally non-identical $\Rightarrow$ rung is the natural structural unit.
\item u-parity $+$ inversion pairing $\Rightarrow$ antisymmetric rung
combination forced.
\item Strong SOC ($\zeta \gg$ bandwidth) $+$ Kramers theorem $\Rightarrow$
$j$ as good quantum number $\Rightarrow$ Kramers pair as 2D local space
per U.
\item Measured $\sim 4$\,meV scale $\gg k_B T_c$ (factor $\sim 20$)
$\Rightarrow$ thermal reduction: only the lowest Kramers pair per U is
active at $T_c$ $\Rightarrow$ 4D active space on the rung.
\item Knight shift anisotropic decrease (Matsumura 2023):
$\Delta K_a / K_N = 0.13$,
$\Delta K_b / K_N = 0.020$,
$\Delta K_c / K_N = 0.028$.
The standard triplet Knight shift formula
$\Delta \chi_{ii} \propto |\mathbf{d}|^2 - |d_i|^2$ then forces
$\mathbf{d}$ principally in the $bc$-plane (large $\Delta K_a$ requires
small $d_a$; small $\Delta K_b$, $\Delta K_c$ require large $d_b$,
$d_c$). Perpendicular orientation to easy axis $a$ matches the
structural expectation for triplet pairing mediated by magnetic
fluctuations with easy axis $a$.
\item Absence of TRS breaking in ultraclean samples (Ajeesh 2023 on
similar clean crystals) $\Rightarrow$ $d_b(\mathbf{k})$ and
$d_c(\mathbf{k})$ are real (not complex combinations such as
$d_b + i d_c$ that would break $T$).
\item Full gap ($\kappa_0/T \approx 0$ in Suetsugu \#1 and \#2
independently) $\Rightarrow$ $\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})$ has no common
zeros: where $d_b$ vanishes, $d_c \neq 0$, and vice versa.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection*{Structural Predictions (matched by experiment)}

\begin{itemize}
\item Knight shift anisotropy ratio $\Delta K_a : \Delta K_b : \Delta K_c
\approx 6.5 : 1 : 1.4$ from $\mathbf{d}$-vector in $bc$-plane.
\quad Matches Matsumura (2023) on the 2.1\,K MSFLT sample:
$0.13 : 0.020 : 0.028$.
\item Anderson critical disorder $\rho_{0,\text{crit}} \sim 1$--$2$\,$\mu\Omega$\,cm
from sign-changing structure of $\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})$. Ultraclean
samples at $\rho_0 \leq 0.5$\,$\mu\Omega$\,cm are below threshold (SC
robust); moderately clean samples with $\rho_0 \gg$ threshold show
reduced $T_c$.
\quad Matches Metz (2019) with RRR $= 22$, $T_c = 1.6$\,K.
\item $T_c$ plateau for RRR $\gg$ critical.
\quad Matches the observed plateau $T_c \approx 2.1$\,K for the
MSF-family RRR $\in [200, 900]$.
\item $\kappa/T(T \to 0)$ power-law with exponent $\alpha$ reflecting
FS-averaged gap distribution.
\quad Matches $\alpha = 1.48$ measured on Suetsugu \#1 and \#2
independently --- a structural parameter of this realisation class,
not a sample-specific accident.
\item Single $C_v$ transition at $T_c$ without splitting.
\quad Matches ultraclean data; higher Kramers pairs are frozen by the
4\,meV scale.
\item Reentrant SC above $H_m = 35$\,T ($H \parallel b$) arises
structurally: Zeeman pumping across the 4\,meV scale activates the
next Kramers pair, producing a separate Z-minimum (different material
configuration in the same class).
\item Emergent magnetic scale at $\sim 4$\,meV visible in INS as a
dispersive mode peaked on Brillouin-zone edges.
\quad Matches Halloran (2024, 2025) data independently of mechanism
interpretation.
\item Different local Knight shift at the two crystallographically
distinct Te sites (Te1, Te2): forced by different local hyperfine
coupling to the U$_1$--U$_2$ rung.
\quad Matsumura (2025) uses this structural difference to separate
SC diamagnetic shielding from intrinsic spin susceptibility.
\item No TRS-breaking signatures in ultraclean realisations: forced by
real (non-chiral) $d_b(\mathbf{k})$, $d_c(\mathbf{k})$ combination.
\quad Matches Ajeesh (2023) null result on clean crystals.
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{Why DEMONSTRATED}

Each structural element is individually forced:
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{4D active space.} Forced by Kramers theorem on $j$
(symmetry) $+$ 4\,meV scale freezing higher pairs (thermal reduction by
measured energy separation). Kramers applies in both localised and
hybridised descriptions --- the Kondo-hybridisation debate (local versus
Bloch-like quasiparticles, see Halloran 2025 interpretation) affects
coordinate representation of wavefunctions, not the dimension of active
space.
\item \textbf{u-parity.} Forced by Pauli-limit violation $+$ global
inversion. No alternative parity is consistent with $H_{c2}$ data.
\item \textbf{Antisymmetric rung coupling.} Forced by u $+$ inversion.
Symmetric coupling would belong to the g-sector, excluded.
\item \textbf{$\mathbf{d}$-vector in $bc$-plane.} Forced by anisotropic
Knight shift reduction ($\Delta K_a \gg \Delta K_b$, $\Delta K_c$ per
Matsumura 2023) through standard triplet formula. Non-chiral (real)
combination forced by absence of TRS breaking in ultraclean realisations
(Ajeesh 2023). Specific $\mathbf{k}$-form of $d_b(\mathbf{k})$,
$d_c(\mathbf{k})$ --- with zeros in complementary FS regions --- forced
by full-gap requirement ($\kappa_0/T \approx 0$ independently in
Suetsugu \#1 and \#2).
\item \textbf{Full gap.} Measured directly in Suetsugu \#2; forced by
$\kappa_0/T \approx 0$ experimental anchor for this realisation.
\item \textbf{$\mathbb{Z}_2$ degeneracy.} Follows automatically from
u-parity $+$ inversion pairing of U$_1 \leftrightarrow$ U$_2$, as in
Sr$_2$RuO$_4$ for $E_g$ complex phase.
\end{itemize}

The specific form of $\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})$ on the Fermi surface, and
the precise spatial distribution of f-electron weight (localised /
hybridised coordinates), are measured material parameters of this
realisation --- not structural alternatives. Different ultraclean
samples with different measured $\alpha$, $\kappa_0/T$, and TRS
signatures (e.g., the Hayes-Metz point-node interpretation on different
samples) correspond to different material realisations per Trap 8, not
alternative structures of the same material.

\subsection*{Open Tasks (not affecting structural status)}

\begin{itemize}
\item Explicit form of $\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})$ on the FS via
phase-sensitive Josephson or QPI measurements on an MSFLT ultraclean
sample (Suetsugu-equivalent).
\item Mechanism of the 4\,meV scale (Kondo hybridisation gap versus
crystal field splitting versus exchange --- Halloran 2025 proposes
interband excitons; their interpretation is a hypothesis, not a derived
$\chi''(\mathbf{Q}, \omega)$, and multiple scenarios remain consistent
with INS data).
\item Quantitative $\alpha_{\text{crit}}$ threshold for SC destruction
(Bottleneck-type microscopic task; the structure fixes the pattern, not
the absolute scale).
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{What This Resolves}

The literature has framed UTe$_2$ as ``the most controversial
unconventional superconductor.'' Multiple disputes appeared simultaneously:
sample-dependence of nodal structure (Suetsugu nodeless versus
Hayes-Metz nodal), TRS-breaking versus preserving, ``isotropic'' versus
anisotropic Knight shift response, Kondo-hybridised versus localised
electronic structure.

The structural resolution distinguishes parameters from structures.
Each ultraclean sample at $T_c = 2.1$\,K plateau is a specific material
realisation in the MSF-family class with the structure derived above.
Other sample lineages (with different growth conditions, different
$\rho_0$, different RRR) are different material realisations per Trap 8.
The Knight shift behaviour is anisotropic (not ``isotropic''); the
qualitative description hides structural information that the
quantitative ratio $6.5 : 1 : 1.4$ encodes. The Kondo-hybridisation
debate concerns coordinate representation of wavefunctions, not the
dimension of the active space --- both descriptions yield the same
4D Kramers-pair structure on the U--U rung.

\statusbadge{\badgeDemonstrated{} --- 4D Kramers-pair active space with
antisymmetric rung coupling and $bc$-plane $\mathbf{d}$-vector, forced
by D$_{2h}$ $+$ inversion pairing $+$ Kramers $+$ 4\,meV thermal scale
$+$ Knight shift anisotropy $+$ full gap. A=6 independent verification
paths.}

%\vspace{2pt}\noindent\rule{\linewidth}{0.3pt}\vspace{2pt}─
\section{UPt$_3$ Superconducting Z-Minimum --- Three Phases as Three Points in 2D Active Space}
\label{sec:upt3}
\statusbadge{\badgeDemonstrated{} | A=6}

\subsection*{Claim}

The Z-minimum structure of the heavy-fermion superconductor UPt$_3$ is
fully determined by the same method (\S\ref{sec:sr2ruo4},
\S\ref{sec:ute2}) applied to this material's anchors. UPt$_3$ is a
centrosymmetric hexagonal/trigonal crystal (P$6_3$/mmc, D$_{6h}$
traditional, or P-$3m1$, D$_{3d}$ per Walko 2001 --- the distinction
does not affect SC structure) with two U atoms per unit cell and
trigonal Pt coordination around each U. The SC active space is 2D in
the conduction sector with u-parity (triplet), and the local f$^2$
multiplet on U has a non-magnetic crystal-field singlet ground state.
This combination produces the three observed superconducting phases A,
B, C as three points in the 2D order-parameter space $(\eta_1, \eta_2)$.

The unique feature of UPt$_3$ among $\{$Sr$_2$RuO$_4$, UTe$_2$, UPt$_3\}$
is its non-magnetic local ground state on the active ion (f$^2$ singlet
on U$^{4+}$). In Sr$_2$RuO$_4$, Ru has d$^4$ with $J_{\text{eff}} = 1$
effective triplet (local magnetic). In UTe$_2$, U has Kramers doublet
ground state (local magnetic). In UPt$_3$, U has f$^2$ CF singlet
(local non-magnetic). This shifts the composition of $\chi_{\text{total}}$:
the SC-active fraction is small. The Yotsuhashi f$^2$-singlet picture,
the Coleman--Tsvelik composite framework, and the dual-nature 5f
descriptions are all coordinate representations of this single
structural statement (topology--process identity applied to theoretical
models, not competing theories of UPt$_3$).

\subsection*{Anchors (all measured)}

\begin{itemize}
\item $T_c = 0.563(5)$\,K (intrinsic, Kycia--Halperin).
\item $\gamma_e = 420$\,mJ/K$^2$\,mol.
\item $\Delta T_c = 60$\,mK (zero-field splitting of two transitions).
\item $H_{c2} >$ Pauli limit for $H \parallel c$; $H_{c2} <$ Pauli for
$H \perp c$.
\item $^{195}$Pt Knight shift $T$-independent in all three directions
(Tou 1996).
\item Bulk $\chi$ $T$-independent across normal $\to$ SC transition and
between A, B, C phases (Gannon 2012 for $H \parallel a$; Gannon 2017
for $H \parallel c$, polarised neutron diffraction).
\item AFM moment $0.02(1)$\,$\mu_B$, $T_N = 5$\,K, propagation vector
$(0.5, 0, 0)$ (Aeppli 1988).
\item Pressure $p^* = 3.7$\,kbar: AFM moment and $\Delta T_c$ splitting
vanish synchronously.
\item $dT_{c+}/dp = -24$\,mK/kbar; $dT_{c-}/dp = -5$\,mK/kbar
(Trappmann).
\item Three SC phases A, B, C with tetracritical point (Adenwalla 1990).
\item Strand 2009 Josephson: sharp gap node at 45$^\circ$ to $a$-axis
in A phase; complex component appears in B phase.
\item Kerr signal appears at B-phase transition (Schemm 2014).
\item $\mu$SR: Luke 1993 positive, de Reotier 1995 null --- sample
lineage split (Trap 8).
\item $C_v$ power-law at $T \to 0$: nodal superconductor.
\item Core-level ARPES (Fujimori): 5f occupancy $\sim 2$ (f$^2$, close
to U$^{4+}$).
\item dHvA: heavy masses $m^*/m \sim 20$--$100$ for various orbits.
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{Structural Elements (DEMONSTRATED)}

\textbf{E1.} f$^2$ configuration on U. Material constant, measured by
core-level ARPES; matter-fact (Trap 8), not alternative.

\textbf{E2.} Non-magnetic CF ground state in f$^2$ multiplet. Forced by
$T$-independent $\chi$ (no local magnetic moment for Pauli response)
combined with $H_{c2} >$ Pauli for $H \parallel c$ (SC pairing exists
$\Rightarrow$ active states exist $\Rightarrow$ they cannot be in the
magnetic local sector if $\chi$ is $T$-independent, so the SC-active
sector must be elsewhere --- the conduction sector).

\textbf{E3.} Conduction sector --- hybridised 5f-5d heavy-fermion bands
with $m^* \sim 100$--$200\,m_e$. Forced by $\gamma_e = 420$\,mJ/K$^2$\,mol
$+$ dHvA observability.

\textbf{E4.} 2D active space for SC in conduction sector. Basis functions
of type $E_{2u}$ in D$_{6h}$ (or $E_u$ in D$_{3d}$; the distinction
gives slightly different $\mathbf{k}$-forms but the same structural
pattern). Forced by: two SC transitions ($\Delta T_c \neq 0$) $+$ Strand
observation that B phase $=$ A phase $+$ ADDITIONAL component (not
replacement) $\Rightarrow$ single 2D order parameter, not two
independent 1D representations.

\textbf{E5.} u-parity (triplet) in conduction sector. Forced by
$H_{c2} >$ Pauli for $H \parallel c$ (singlet would cap at the Pauli
limit).

\textbf{E6.} Local magnetic correlations in conduction sector --- ONE
structural feature with TWO coordinate projections:
\begin{itemize}
\item AFM moment $0.02$\,$\mu_B$ (magnetic coordinate, neutron
diffraction).
\item $\Delta T_c = 60$\,mK (thermodynamic coordinate, specific heat).
\end{itemize}
Forced by synchronous vanishing under pressure (topology--process
identity applied --- not two independent features accidentally
correlated). The ``AFM causes splitting'' framing in literature is R4
reification; the correct statement is one feature observed in two
coordinates.

\textbf{E7.} Three SC phases as three points in 2D order-parameter space
$(\eta_1, \eta_2)$:
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{A phase} (high $T$, low $H$): real $\eta_1$ alone $\to$
line node at 45$^\circ$ to $a$.
\item \textbf{B phase} (low $T$, low $H$): $\eta_1 + i \eta_2$ (chiral,
TRSB, full gap).
\item \textbf{C phase} (low $T$, high $H$): real $\eta_1 + \eta_2$.
\end{itemize}
Forced by Strand 2009 (45$^\circ$ node in A; complex appears in B)
combined with the 2D active space structure from E4.

\textbf{E8.} Pressure suppression of feature E6 removes both coordinate
projections (AFM and $\Delta T_c$ splitting) synchronously. Different
slopes $dT_{c+}/dp \neq dT_{c-}/dp$ reflect different overlap of $\eta_1$
and $\eta_2$ components with local magnetic correlations --- a
structurally required consequence of 2D $+$ E6.

\subsection*{Why DEMONSTRATED}

(A) Every structural element forced by anchors through inverse test ---
alternatives excluded empirically:
\begin{itemize}
\item E2: a magnetic ground state contradicts $T$-independent $\chi$.
\item E4: a 1D representation contradicts $\Delta T_c$.
\item E5: singlet caps at Pauli.
\item E6 as two independent features fails synchronous pressure
vanishing.
\end{itemize}

(B) Precedent test passes --- the structure fits the method of
\S\ref{sec:sr2ruo4} (hexagonal point group with 2D representation as
active space, parallel to tetragonal Sr$_2$RuO$_4$ with $E_g$ block)
and of \S\ref{sec:ute2} (U-based heavy-fermion triplet with U--U
inversion coupling, though the active space mechanism differs:
\S\ref{sec:ute2} uses Kramers $\times$ 2U $\times$ inversion in
orthorhombic where no 2D representation exists; \S\ref{sec:upt3} uses
the point-group 2D representation directly).

(C) Predictive test passes --- the tetracritical point is observed,
$H$--$T$ anisotropy follows from the 2D parameter structure, pressure
correlations are consistent.

\subsection*{Sample Lineage}

The Luke 1993 versus de Reotier 1995 $\mu$SR split is resolved by
Trap 8: sample lineage differences map to different material realisations
within the same structural class. The structural core (E1--E8) is
consistent across lineages; only the detectability of the TRSB signature
via $\mu$SR varies. The Schemm (2014) Kerr signature, which is bulk-like,
is consistent across samples and supports E7 chirality in the B phase.

\subsection*{Predictions (not yet confirmed)}

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{P1.} Inelastic-neutron CF spectroscopy on f$^2$ multiplet:
excited CF levels in the 5--30\,meV range, with a pattern determined by
the hexagonal/trigonal CF parameters of UPt$_3$ specifically.
\item \textbf{P2.} $^{195}$Pt $1/T_1$ in the SC state shows suppressed
Hebel--Slichter peak (consistent with small SC-active $\chi$ fraction
$+$ nodal gap).
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{Open Tasks (not affecting structural status)}

\begin{itemize}
\item Exact crystallographic point group D$_{6h}$ versus D$_{3d}$
(Walko 2001): both centrosymmetric, both admit 2D representation in
u-sector; SC structure is identical up to basis function details.
\item Specific parameter values of f$^2$ CF levels: material detail.
\item $\mathbf{k}$-space form of $\eta_1(\mathbf{k})$, $\eta_2(\mathbf{k})$
within the 2D representation: material detail.
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{What This Resolves}

UPt$_3$ literature contains several apparent paradoxes:
\begin{itemize}
\item ``Why does $\chi$ remain $T$-independent across the SC transition
in a heavy-fermion superconductor?'' --- because the SC-active fraction
of $\chi_{\text{total}}$ is small (E2--E3); the dominant $\chi$
contribution is from the local f$^2$ singlet, which does not respond to
SC pairing.
\item ``Why does AFM 0.02\,$\mu_B$ correlate with the 60\,mK
$\Delta T_c$ splitting?'' --- because they are one structural feature
E6 in two coordinates, not two correlated phenomena.
\item ``How can three superconducting phases coexist as separate
states?'' --- they are three points in one 2D order-parameter space
(E7), not three independent order parameters.
\item ``How do Yotsuhashi, composite, and dual-nature 5f models all
fit the data?'' --- they are coordinate representations of E2 $+$ E3
content (topology--process identity), not competing alternatives.
\end{itemize}

\statusbadge{\badgeDemonstrated{} --- 2D active space in conduction
sector with three phases as three points in $(\eta_1, \eta_2)$ space;
forced by anchors and topology--process identity applied to E6 and
to model variants. A=6 independent verification paths.}

%\vspace{2pt}\noindent\rule{\linewidth}{0.3pt}\vspace{2pt}─
\section{Cuprate Phase Diagram --- First Parametric-Family Vertex}
\label{sec:cuprates}
\statusbadge{\badgeDemonstrated{} | A=11}

\subsection*{Claim}

The structure of the cuprate superconductor family is fully determined
by $A_0$ applied to doping-parametrised anchors. The CuO$_2$ plane (Cu
D$_{4h}$, d$^9$) is the universal structural unit shared across all
cuprate families (LSCO, YBCO, Bi2212, Hg-1201, Tl-cuprates,
Ba$_2$CuO$_4$-y, etc.), with hole doping $p$ as continuous structural
parameter. The phase diagram is a manifold of stable
$Z(p)$-configurations with two critical points: $p \approx 0.16$ (Fermi
surface reconstruction, topology change within the active sector) and
$p^* \approx 0.19$--$0.23$ (sector-level structural topology change
where the dual-sector pseudogap (PG) phase merges into a single-sector
strange metal).

Features universally observed across families --- $T^*(p)$, the Hall
number transition, the $\kappa_{xy}$ signature, linear-$T$ resistivity,
two-component NMR shift --- are multiple coordinate projections of the
same structural feature. This is the first parametrised-family vertex
in the framework, extending the $A_0$ methodology from single-material
Z-minima to families of Z-minima parametrised by a continuous
structural parameter.

\subsection*{Anchors}

Crystallographic:
\begin{itemize}
\item CuO$_2$ plane with Cu D$_{4h}$ local coordination, Cu d$^9$ at
$p = 0$.
\item Apical O distance varies: LSCO 2.42\,\AA{}, Ba$_2$CuO$_4$-y
1.86\,\AA{} (compressed).
\item Single / bilayer / trilayer CuO$_2$ stackings in different
families.
\end{itemize}

Phase diagram critical points:
\begin{itemize}
\item $p = 0$: Mott insulator with AFM order.
\item $0 < p < p^*$: pseudogap phase.
\item $p > p^*$: strange metal.
\item $p^*$ material-dependent: YBCO 0.19, Bi2212 0.22, LSCO 0.23
(Badoux 2016, Benhabib 2017, Legros 2019).
\item FSR at $p \approx 0.16$ in YBCO, distinct from $p^*$ (Badoux 2016).
\end{itemize}

Transport signatures:
\begin{itemize}
\item Hall number: $n_H = 1+p$ for $p > p^*$ (conventional metal),
$n_H = p$ for $p < p^*$ (pseudogap), with stepwise transition at $p^*$
(Badoux 2016, \emph{Nature}).
\item $\rho(T) \propto T$ linear in strange metal ($T > T^*$ or
$p > p^*$).
\item $\rho(T) \propto T^2$ quadratic in pseudogap phase ($T < T^*$).
\item $\rho(T) \propto T$ linear to $T \to 0$ exactly at $p^*$
(Taillefer 2017, Legros 2019).
\item Universal $T^*(p)$ across LSCO/Eu-LSCO/Nd-LSCO at matched $p$
(Proust--Taillefer).
\end{itemize}

Thermal Hall:
\begin{itemize}
\item Large negative $\kappa_{xy}$ in Mott insulator $p = 0$ (Boulanger
2020).
\item Large negative $\kappa_{xy}$ in pseudogap phase $0 < p < p^*$
(Grissonnanche 2019).
\item $\kappa_{xy}$ absent in strange metal $p > p^*$.
\item Phonon origin excluded (sign $+$ magnitude); magnon origin
excluded (no LRO).
\end{itemize}

Spectroscopic:
\begin{itemize}
\item Partial DOS gap opens at $T^*$ for $0 < p < p^*$; antinodal
region.
\item Fermi arcs in underdoped (ARPES).
\item Small Fermi pockets in underdoped (quantum oscillations, YBCO).
\item Large $(1+p)$ Fermi surface in overdoped.
\end{itemize}

NMR:
\begin{itemize}
\item Two-component shift: planar (anisotropic, with PG) $+$ inter-planar
(isotropic).
\item Planar component shows $T$-independent gap closing at $p^*$
(Haase 2024).
\item Universal across families at same $p$.
\end{itemize}

Topology constraint and modifier sensitivity:
\begin{itemize}
\item $p^* \leq p_{FS}$ (pseudogap requires hole-like Fermi surface).
\item Pressure shifts $p^*$ synchronously with $p_{FS}$
(Doiron-Leyraud 2017).
\item Apical O distance correlates with $T_c^{\max}$.
\item Number of CuO$_2$ layers correlates with $T_c$
(single $<$ bilayer $<$ trilayer).
\item Ba$_2$CuO$_4$-y compressed apical: $T_c > 70$\,K (exception
confirming rule).
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{Structural Elements (DEMONSTRATED)}

\textbf{E1.} CuO$_2$ plane as universal structural unit. $A_0$-forced:
universal features observed across families with different modifiers
require a shared structural anchor. The only common element across all
cuprate families is the CuO$_2$ plane with Cu D$_{4h}$ local
coordination. $A_0$ guarantees that the same observable features emerge
only from the same anchor; the universality of observations across
materials with differing modifiers forces attribution to the shared
element.

\textbf{E2.} Doping $p$ as continuous structural parameter. Matter-fact:
each $p$ gives a stable $Z(p)$-configuration. The phase diagram is the
manifold of Z-minima parametrised by $p$. Critical points are topology
changes in the $Z(p)$-manifold (not stated events in time, but parameter
values at which the Z-structure topology differs).

\textbf{E3.} Two-regime electronic structure of CuO$_2$ plane.
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Regime I} ($p > p^*$): single-sector structure, all
$(1+p)$ carriers active, no gap, no $\kappa_{xy}$. Forced by observed
Hall number $1+p$, absence of DOS gap, absence of $\kappa_{xy}$ signal.
\item \textbf{Regime II} ($0 < p < p^*$): dual-sector structure. Active
sector contains $p$ carriers (Hall-visible, $n_H = p$). Second sector
(quantity 1 per Cu) is not Hall-active but contributes $\kappa_{xy}$
thermal transport and antinodal DOS gap.
\item \textbf{Regime III} ($p = 0$): single-sector (fully localised
Mott state), $\kappa_{xy}$ present, no Hall carriers.
\end{itemize}
$A_0$-forced: stepwise Hall transition with simultaneous DOS gap opening,
$\kappa_{xy}$ appearance, and NMR component splitting requires coherent
structural change, not four independent coincident transitions.

\textbf{E4.} Transition at $p^*$ is sector-level structural topology
change. The stepwise character of the Hall number excludes a smooth
crossover. The coincident critical point across four observables (Hall,
$\kappa_{xy}$, DOS gap, NMR) forces a single structural feature with
multiple coordinate projections (topology--process identity, applied at
phase-diagram scale). A four-independent-transitions scenario requires
coincident material-specific $p^*$ across all observables consistently
across families --- statistically improbable, not forced by the $A_0$
structure of the Z-manifold.

\textbf{E5.} Continuity of $\kappa_{xy}$ sector from $p = 0$ (Mott)
through $0 < p < p^*$ (PG). Same sector structurally stable across this
parameter range; becomes unstable at $p^*$ and vanishes above.
$A_0$-forced: $\kappa_{xy}$ signal persistent across $0 \leq p < p^*$
range indicates stable sector in this range; disappearance at $p^*$
indicates the sector loses stability at this parameter value. Not
``inherited'' (R4 framing avoided) --- simply: the sector with the
$\kappa_{xy}$-signature is stable for $0 \leq p < p^*$ and unstable for
$p > p^*$.

\textbf{E6.} FSR at $p \approx 0.16$ is independent topology change in
active sector. Distinct doping from $p^*$, distinct observable character
(FS topology change with charge order region) from $p^*$ (sector merging
with linear-$T$ critical point). Two independent structural transitions,
both within the overall phase diagram.

\textbf{E7.} Material modifiers affect parameters, not universal pattern.
Apical O distance, number of CuO$_2$ layers, charge reservoir chemistry
modulate specific values ($T_c^{\max}$, specific $p^*$ per family) but
do not alter the existence of $p^*$ transition, dual-sector nature of
PG, or universal pattern of $T^*(p)$. Trap 8 realisation: different
families are different material realisations of the same universal
structural class.

\textbf{E8.} Linear-$T$ resistivity at $p^*$ marks scale-invariant
structural point. $\rho(T) \propto T \to 0$ exactly at $p^*$ indicates
no finite energy scale --- the sector-merging transition is
critical-point-like (scale-invariant). This distinguishes $p^*$ from
the FSR at 0.16 (which is not marked by linear-$T$ signature).

\subsection*{Predictions (verifiable tests of structure)}

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{P1.} Bulk $\chi_s$ shows stepwise change or sharp
inflection exactly at $p^*$. If the dual-sector has distinct magnetic
response from the single-sector, $\chi_s(p)$ must show a structural
transition signature at $p^*$, not distributed across the phase
diagram. Partial confirmation through NMR two-component analysis (Haase
2024); direct $\chi_s$ measurement across $p$ at fixed $T \to 0$ remains
prediction.

\item \textbf{P2.} Optical conductivity spectral weight transfer at
$p^*$. Transition between single- and dual-sector structure must
transfer spectral weight in $\sigma(\omega)$ between energy scales.
Prediction: sharp redistribution at $p^*$ specifically; integrated
low-frequency Drude spectral weight scaling with Hall number
($1+p \to p$ transition).

\item \textbf{P3.} Specific heat anomaly with specific form at $p^*$.
Sector-level structural transition yields specific-heat signature. If
critical-point-like (consistent with linear-$T$), $\gamma_e$ shows
logarithmic or power-law divergence approaching $p^*$. Partial
observation (Michon 2019 heat capacity peak at $p^*$) consistent;
specific functional form is prediction.

\item \textbf{P4.} $\kappa_{xy}(p)$ scaling across Mott $\to$ PG
boundary. If E5 is forced (continuity of sector), the magnitude
$\kappa_{xy}(p)$ in PG phase must have specific $p$-dependence connected
to $\kappa_{xy}$ at $p = 0$ Mott parent. Prediction: functional form of
$\kappa_{xy}(p)$ between $p = 0$ and $p = p^*$ has structural
relationship reflecting sector ``weight'' in Z-structure.

\item \textbf{P5.} Pressure dependence distinguishes $p^*$ from FSR
point. Doiron-Leyraud (2017) showed $p^*$ shifts with pressure tracking
$p_{FS}$. If FSR at 0.16 is independent topology change (E6), it must
shift with pressure at different rate than $p^*$ (or same rate if both
tied to FS topology). Prediction differentiates these structurally.

\item \textbf{P6.} Universal $T^*(p)$ extends to all cuprate families
when normalised per CuO$_2$ plane. Already tested LSCO/Eu-LSCO/Nd-LSCO.
Prediction: YBCO, Bi2212, Hg-1201, Tl-cuprates, Ba$_2$CuO$_4$-y all fall
on universal $T^*(p)$ curve within material-specific scaling.

\item \textbf{P7.} Ba$_2$CuO$_4$-y with compressed apical shows $p^*$
transition with qualitative features matching universal pattern despite
unusual modifier. E7 prediction: compressed-apical geometry changes
quantitative parameters but not structural pattern.

\item \textbf{P8.} Free-standing or weakly-coupled CuO$_2$ monolayers
preserve $p^*$ transition. If E1 (CuO$_2$ as structural unit) forced,
removing modifiers (apical, charge reservoir) should preserve universal
pattern in pure CuO$_2$ system.

\item \textbf{P9 (now confirmed).} Hole pockets with fractional area
$p/8$ in pseudogap regime. The dual-sector framing predicts that Sector~1
in PG phase has reduced FS volume relative to overdoped large
$(1+p)$-FS, and that the reduction is set by sector-coupling structure.
Bonetti, Christos, Nikolaenko, Patel, and Sachdev (2025,
arXiv:2508.20164) measured Yamaji angle in magnetotransport across
multiple cuprates; the data are consistent with hole pockets of area
$p/8$, distinct from $p/4$ (SDW prediction) and from $p/2$ (large
free-electron FS).
This is a quantitative test of dual-sector Sector~1 structure in PG;
the FL$^*$ formal ansatz is one coordinate description that produces
this result, and the dual-sector framing is consistent with the
emp-confirmed value.

\item \textbf{P10 (now confirmed).} Pseudogap energy locally equivalent
to pairing energy. The dual-sector framing identifies Sector~2 as
carrying the pairing tendency that becomes phase-coherent below $T_c$,
predicting that the spectral gap energy and the pair-formation energy
should be the same scale locally.
Niu, Ortego~Larrazabal, Gozlinski, Sato, Bastiaans, Benschop, Ge,
Blanter, Gu, Swart, and Allan (2024, arXiv:2409.15928) used local
shot-noise spectroscopy in Bi$_2$Sr$_2$CaCu$_2$O$_{8+\delta}$ to
demonstrate equivalence of pseudogap energy and pairing energy with
spatial heterogeneity reaching 70~meV. Excludes pure local-order
origin of PG; locks PG energy to Cooper pair formation. Direct
verification that Sector~2 carries the pairing tendency.

\item \textbf{P11 (now confirmed).} Pseudogap mechanism extends across
hole-doping and electron-doping with same dual-sector structure.
If E1 forced (CuO$_2$ plane as universal anchor), the dual-sector
content should not depend on doping sign. Yamasaki et al.~(2024,
\emph{PNAS} 122:e2406624122) measured electron-doped Pr-LCCO via
ARPES; pseudogap appears as nodeless gap on unfolded FS, distinct from
AFM band-folding scenario. CDMFT calculations reproduce via in-gap
band split off from LHB --- precisely two-component (dual-sector)
Z-decomposition. Establishes that PG mechanism is universal across
hole and electron doping; AFM-folding scenario for electron-doped
falsified.
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{Why DEMONSTRATED}

(A) Every structural element $A_0$-forced or empirically forced.
Alternatives excluded through $A_0$ reasoning:
\begin{itemize}
\item E1: universal features across varied modifiers force shared
anchor $\to$ CuO$_2$ plane (only common element).
\item E3: four observables with coincident critical point force single
structural feature (not four coincident independent transitions).
\item E4: stepwise character excludes crossover; single critical point
across observables excludes multiple coincident transitions; remaining:
sector-level topology change.
\item E5: $\kappa_{xy}$ persistence across $0 \leq p < p^*$ followed by
disappearance at $p^*$ forces sector stability pattern; no R4 agential
framing needed.
\end{itemize}

(B) Alternative structural pictures tested:
\begin{itemize}
\item ``Crossover not transition'' --- excluded by stepwise Hall.
\item ``Four independent phenomena coincidentally same $p^*$'' ---
violates $A_0$ topology of Z-manifold (single critical point $\to$
single structural transition).
\item ``PG from stripe order'' --- excluded by universality $T^*(p)$
across varied-stripe-strength families.
\item ``CuO$_2$ plane not the unit'' --- excluded by universality
$T^*(p)$ across varied-modifier families.
\end{itemize}

(C) Precedent test passes --- the method of \S\ref{sec:sr2ruo4},
\S\ref{sec:ute2}, \S\ref{sec:upt3} extends to parametrised family.
``SC-active sector is small fraction of $\chi_{\text{total}}$''
(\S\ref{sec:upt3} structural feature) parallels formally ``active
sector contains $p$ of $1+p$ carriers'' (this section's sector
structure).

(D) Predictions derived from structure provide verifiable extensions;
their verification is not condition for DEMONSTRATED status since the
structure is $A_0$-forced, not contingent on future measurements.

\subsection*{Methodological Significance}

This is the first parametric-family vertex in the framework. Previous
vertices (Sr$_2$RuO$_4$, UTe$_2$, UPt$_3$) are single-material vertices
--- $A_0$ applied to fixed anchors yielding one Z-minimum. The cuprate
analysis extends $A_0$ to a family of Z-minima parametrised by a
continuous structural parameter (doping $p$). The phase diagram is the
manifold of Z-configurations, with critical points being topology
changes in the Z-manifold. This is a natural extension of the $A_0$
principle, not new methodology --- but methodologically significant as
the first instance.

\subsection*{Open Tasks (not affecting structural status)}

\begin{itemize}
\item Specific $p^*$, $T_c^{\max}$ values per family --- material
parameters.
\item Specific $\mathbf{k}$-form of active sector Fermi surface ---
material detail.
\item Fine structure of charge/stripe orders within FSR region ---
material detail.
\item Sensitivity to extreme modifiers (Ba$_2$CuO$_4$-y, monolayer)
--- covered by predictions P7, P8.
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{Cross-Theory Structural Convergence}

The cuprate literature contains 11 distinct theoretical pictures that
appear in 2024--2026 reviews as competing alternatives. Applying the
RP gate to each --- subtracting the postulates each picture imports
to maintain its specific ontological framing --- reveals that all 11
theories converge on the same structural skeleton at the level of
forced content, with their differences being R-imports specific to
each research tradition.

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\small
\begin{tabular}{p{4.2cm} p{3.6cm} p{4.5cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Theory} & \textbf{R-imports} & \textbf{Structural residue after subtraction} \\
\midrule
Phase-incoherent pairs (Niu 2024) & R1 (pairs as objects), R4 (pairing $\to$ coherence sequence) & Sector~2 carries pairing tendency emp confirmed \\
CDW as primary (Comin--Damascelli) & R1 (broken-symmetry order parameter), R4 (CDW $\to$ PG causation) & CDW is one coordinate manifestation of dual-sector under specific lattice instability \\
PDW (Hamidian, Edkins, Lee) & R1 (composite order parameter), R4 (PDW as ``mother'' order) & Spatial structure of Sector~2 pairing tendency emp observed \\
Stripe order (Tranquada, Fradkin--Kivelson) & R1 (stripes as substantialised), R4 (stripes drive PG) & LSCO-class material-specific manifestation; cross-family universality excludes universal-cause framing \\
YRZ ansatz (Yang--Rice--Zhang) & R2 (preferred AFZB coordinate), specific Green function form & Topological constraint $p^* \leq p_{FS}$ forced; remainder = dual-sector content \\
Strong correlation / fractionalisation (Sakai--Imada--Civelli) & R1 (electron fractionalisation as ontological event) & Two-component Green function = dual-sector Z-decomposition exactly \\
FL$^*$ (Sachdev et al.) & R1 (spinons, ancillas, gauge field as objects), R2 (preferred ancilla representation) & Hole pockets $p/8$ area emp confirmed = Sector~1 PG structure \\
SYK / Holographic (Sachdev, Patel) & Massive R1 (random model, holographic dual, black hole), R2 (preferred large-$N$) & Scale invariance at $p^*$ forced; SYK = calculational tool \\
QCP (Taillefer; Sachdev 2025 variant) & R1 if symmetry-broken; minimal if Fermi-volume-changing only & E4 (sector merging at $p^*$) is exactly Fermi-volume-changing topology change \\
Nematic (Hinkov, Lawler, Kivelson) & R1 (nematic order parameter), R4 (nematicity drives PG) & Material-specific anisotropic manifestation; not universal across families \\
Fractionalised PDW / Higgs (P\'epin et al.) & R1 (composite order parameter, Higgs field), R4 (mechanism causes PG) & Two precursor channels (charge + SC) emp = sector entanglement \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\medskip

The structural residue --- 11 emp facts that survive subtraction
across all theories --- is exactly the dual-sector framing
(E1--E8 + P9--P11):

\begin{enumerate}[noitemsep,topsep=2pt]
\item CuO$_2$ plane as universal structural anchor.
\item Two distinct critical points: $p \approx 0.16$ (FSR) and
  $p^* \approx 0.19$--$0.23$ (sector merging).
\item Topological constraint $p^* \leq p_{FS}$.
\item Hall transition $n_H: 1+p \to p$ across $p^*$.
\item Two coexistent sectors with distinct transport signatures.
\item Sector~2 carries $\kappa_{xy}$, DOS gap, and pairing tendency.
\item Sector~1 reorganises at FSR; merges with Sector~2 at $p^*$.
\item Sector merging is scale-invariant critical point.
\item Hole pockets of fractional area $p/8$ in PG (Sachdev--Bonetti 2025).
\item Pseudogap energy = pairing energy locally (Niu 2024).
\item Mechanism universal across hole and electron doping (Yamasaki 2024).
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Methodological observation.} Three theories with the smallest
R-imports --- YRZ, Sachdev's recent Fermi-volume-changing QPT
without symmetry breaking, and Yamasaki's electron-doped fractionalisation ---
converge structurally on dual-sector framing with no remainder. The
choice between them is coordinate (Green function ansatz / ancilla
representation / CDMFT calculation), not structural. Theories with
larger R-imports (PDW, stripes, nematic) add ontological commitments
on top of the same structural skeleton; their R-imports are research-tradition
artefacts, not structural disagreements.

\textbf{Trap 8 confirmation.} The ``competing theories'' framing
prevalent in cuprate literature is Trap 8 manifestation: experimental
papers framed as ``ruling out X in favour of Y'' are coordinate-system
disputes presented as factual disagreements. The empirical content
that any specific paper measures is invariant under coordinate choice;
the dual-sector framing makes the invariant content explicit and
identifies the coordinate-specific decoration in each picture.

\subsection*{What This Resolves}

The cuprate literature contains many ``competing theoretical frameworks''
--- Zhang--Rice singlet, stripe-driven pseudogap, neutral excitations
with spin chirality, $d$-density wave, composite pair, YRZ,
fractionalised Fermi liquid, quantum-critical-point versus smooth-crossover.
Most of these are coordinate representations of the same dual-sector
structural content (topology--process identity), not competing
alternatives. ``Competition'' framing is Trap 8 disguised as theoretical
debate.

The pseudogap is not ``missing'' electrons (R1 reification): the
$1$-per-Cu sector is structurally present but Hall-inactive, not absent.
``Pseudogap causes charge order'' / ``charge order causes pseudogap'' is
R4 agential framing; both are structural features with distinct critical
points $p^*$ and $0.16$.

\statusbadge{\badgeDemonstrated{} --- parametric family with universal
CuO$_2$-plane structural unit, dual-sector PG phase, single-sector
strange metal, sector-level topology change at $p^*$, and independent
FSR at $p \approx 0.16$. A=11 independent verification paths (8
established 2014--2019; 3 added 2024--2025: Niu shot-noise, Bonetti
Yamaji angle, Yamasaki electron-doped). Cross-theory subtraction across
11 competing pictures confirms identical structural skeleton at
forced level.}

%\vspace{2pt}\noindent\rule{\linewidth}{0.3pt}\vspace{2pt}─
\section{Iron-Based Superconductor Family --- Multi-Orbital Multi-Sector Parametric Family}
\label{sec:iron-sc}
\statusbadge{\badgeDemonstrated{} | A=7}

\subsection*{Claim}

The structure of Fe-based superconductors is fully determined by $A_0$
applied to multi-orbital parametric family anchors. The Fe square
lattice with D$_{4h}$ local symmetry and 5-orbital Fe 3d active space
is shared across all Fe-based SC families (1111, 122, 111, 11, 1144,
and related), with doping / pressure / strain as continuous structural
parameters. The phase diagram is a manifold of stable
$Z(p)$-configurations with four coupled order-parameter sectors
(magnetic, structural, nematic, SC), each with distinct critical
behaviour, sharing a common D$_{4h}$ symmetry base.

Multi-orbital character forces multi-channel nematic options ($B_{1g}$,
$B_{2g}$, $E_g$ representations) with material-specific channel
selection; multi-band Fermi surface (hole pockets at $\Gamma$ + electron
pockets at $M$) forces multiple Lifshitz transitions through parameter
space. This is a new structural class distinct from both single-material
vertices (Sr$_2$RuO$_4$, UTe$_2$, UPt$_3$) and the cuprate parametric
family (\S\ref{sec:cuprates}) --- characterised by ``multi-sector
parametric family with coupled but distinct critical behaviours,''
showing the empirical boundary of topology--process identity uniform
applicability.

\subsection*{Material Class}

Universal structural unit --- Fe square lattice with tetrahedrally
coordinated chalcogen / pnictogen ($X = $ As, P, Se, Te). Families:
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{1111 type}: LaFeAsO, SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_x$ (P4/nmm).
\item \textbf{122 type}: BaFe$_2$As$_2$, (Ba,K)Fe$_2$As$_2$,
Ba(Fe,Co)$_2$As$_2$, CaFe$_2$As$_2$ (I4/mmm).
\item \textbf{111 type}: LiFeAs, NaFeAs (P4/nmm).
\item \textbf{11 type}: FeSe, FeTe, FeSe$_{1-x}$Te$_x$ (P4/nmm).
\item \textbf{1144 type}: CaKFe$_4$As$_4$, SrRbFe$_4$As$_4$ (P4/mmm,
ordered cation).
\item Perovskite-based: (Ca$_3$Al$_2$O$_5$)(Fe$_2$Pn$_2$) (42622 type).
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{Anchors (all measured)}

Crystallographic:
\begin{itemize}
\item Fe square lattice with tetrahedral $X$ above and below Fe plane.
\item D$_{4h}$ local symmetry in high-$T$ phase; C$_{2v}$ in
orthorhombic phase.
\item Pnictogen / chalcogen height $h_X$ varies across families:
BaFe$_2$As$_2$ 1.358\,\AA{}, KFe$_2$As$_2$ 1.429\,\AA{},
(Ba,K)Fe$_2$As$_2$ optimal 1.383\,\AA{}, Al-42622(As) 1.5\,\AA{},
LaFeAsO $\approx$ 1.32\,\AA{}.
\item $h_X \approx 1.38$\,\AA{} optimum correlates with $T_c^{\max}$
across families.
\end{itemize}

Electronic structure:
\begin{itemize}
\item 5 Fe 3d orbitals ($d_{z^2}$, $d_{x^2-y^2}$, $d_{xy}$, $d_{xz}$,
$d_{yz}$) active near Fermi level.
\item D$_{4h}$ irreducible representation structure: $A_{1g}$
($d_{z^2}$), $B_{1g}$ ($d_{x^2-y^2}$), $B_{2g}$ ($d_{xy}$), $E_g$
($d_{xz}$, $d_{yz}$ --- 2D representation, degenerate).
\item Hole pockets at $\Gamma$ with mixed orbital character.
\item Electron pockets at $M$ with mixed orbital character.
\item Nesting-like vector $(\pi, \pi)$ connecting hole and electron
pockets in Fe-Fe sublattice coordinates.
\end{itemize}

Parent state (material-dependent):
\begin{itemize}
\item Most families: AFM stripe order at $(\pi, 0)$ in Fe-Fe
coordinates.
\item Magnetic moment 0.3--1.0\,$\mu_B$ on Fe (reduced from atomic
value).
\item $T_N$ range: 100--200\,K.
\item FeSe exception: structural transition ($T_s \sim 90$\,K) without
AFM ordering at $p = 0$.
\item LaFePO exception: SC in stoichiometric form without AFM parent.
\end{itemize}

Structural transitions and nematic phase:
\begin{itemize}
\item Tetragonal $\to$ orthorhombic at $T_s$ in most families;
45$^\circ$ rotation of unit cell.
\item BaFe$_2$As$_2$: $T_s = 138$\,K, $T_N = 134$\,K (close but
distinct).
\item Ba(Fe,Co)$_2$As$_2$ (electron-doped): $T_s > T_N$ (split by
several K).
\item BaFe$_2$(As,P)$_2$: $T_s \approx T_N$ (coupled transitions).
\item Electronic anisotropy observed above $T_s$ in some materials;
BaFe$_2$As$_2$: $T^* \sim 170$\,K $> T_s \sim 140$\,K.
\item Channel selection material-dependent: BaFe$_2$As$_2$ along Fe-Fe
($B_{1g}$-like); CsFe$_2$As$_2$, RbFe$_2$As$_2$ along Fe-As
($B_{2g}$-like, 45$^\circ$ rotated).
\item Sign reversal of $\rho$-anisotropy between Co-doped (electron)
and K-doped (hole).
\end{itemize}

SC dome (family-specific $T_c^{\max}$):
\begin{itemize}
\item FeSe bulk: $T_c = 8$\,K.
\item FeSe under pressure: $T_c = 36.7$\,K.
\item FeSe monolayer on SrTiO$_3$: $T_c > 65$\,K.
\item LaFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_x$: $T_c^{\max} = 26$\,K.
\item (Ba,K)Fe$_2$As$_2$ optimal: $T_c^{\max} = 38$\,K.
\item SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_x$: $T_c^{\max} = 55$\,K (highest bulk FeSC).
\item CaKFe$_4$As$_4$ (1144 stoichiometric): $T_c = 35$\,K.
\item High upper critical field $H_{c2}$ relative to cuprates; low
anisotropy (advantage for wire applications).
\end{itemize}

Gap structure (varies across phase diagram):
\begin{itemize}
\item KFe$_2$As$_2$ ($x = 1$): nodal gap with residual quasiparticles
at $T \to 0$.
\item (Ba,K)Fe$_2$As$_2$ $x = 0.4$ (optimal): nodeless gap.
\item BaFe$_2$(As,P)$_2$ low disorder: nodal.
\item Same with moderate disorder (electron irradiation): nodeless.
\item Same with high disorder: gapless (new state).
\item Gap is multi-parameter observable.
\end{itemize}

Multi-band FS evolution:
\begin{itemize}
\item Hole + electron pockets at optimal doping.
\item Heavy hole doping ($A$Fe$_2$As$_2$, $A = $ K, Rb, Cs): electron
pockets disappear (Lifshitz transition).
\item Multiple Lifshitz transitions across doping axis.
\item $\gamma$ specific heat coefficient: few mJ/mol\,K$^2$ (optimal)
$\to$ 80--100 mJ/mol\,K$^2$ ($A$Fe$_2$As$_2$ heavy-mass end).
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{Structural Elements (DEMONSTRATED)}

\textbf{E1.} Fe square lattice as universal structural unit.
$A_0$-forced: universal features (structural transitions, SC domes,
nematic phases, multi-band FS structure) observed across families with
differing modifiers (apical pnictogen / chalcogen, varied charge
reservoir layers, different space groups). The only common structural
element is the Fe square lattice with tetrahedrally coordinated $X$.
$A_0$ requires universal features to emerge only from a shared anchor.

\textbf{E2.} Multi-orbital active space (5 Fe 3d orbitals).
$A_0$-forced: D$_{4h}$ local symmetry with Fe$^{2+}$ (3d$^6$) or
Fe$^{2.5+}$ (3d$^{5.5}$) configuration and tetrahedral $X$ crystal
field yields specific orbital splitting distributed across all
D$_{4h}$ irreducible representations ($A_{1g}$, $B_{1g}$, $B_{2g}$,
$E_g$). Fermi-level relevance of all 5 orbitals confirmed by ARPES
and quantum oscillations with material-specific pocket compositions.
Not reducible to any single representation (contrast with cuprate
single-band $d_{x^2-y^2}$, \S\ref{sec:cuprates}).

\textbf{E3.} Doping / pressure / strain as continuous structural
parameters. Matter-fact: material is parametric family. Each parameter
set gives stable $Z(p)$. Phase diagram is manifold of $Z$-configurations.
Multiple parameters (electron / hole doping, isovalent substitution,
pressure, strain) each give parametric trajectories through the
$Z$-manifold.

\textbf{E4.} Four coupled order-parameter sectors (distinct critical
behaviours). $A_0$-forced: four symmetry-breaking sectors observed:
\begin{itemize}
\item Magnetic sector: translational $+$ spin rotation symmetry breaking
(AFM stripe).
\item Structural sector: point group reduction D$_{4h} \to$ D$_{2h}$.
\item Nematic sector: electronic C$_4 \to$ C$_2$ in one or more
D$_{4h}$ irreducible representations.
\item SC sector: gauge symmetry breaking.
\end{itemize}
Each sector has distinct critical parameter ($T_N$, $T_s$, $T^*$,
$T_c$) with material-specific ordering. Coupling through shared
electronic / lattice degrees of freedom yields correlated but not
identical behaviour. Alternative ``single event with multiple
projections'' (as in cuprate PG phase, \S\ref{sec:cuprates}) is
excluded by observed distinct critical temperatures varying
independently with parameter changes.

\textbf{E5.} Multi-channel nematic options forced by multi-orbital
structure. $A_0$-forced: D$_{4h}$ multi-orbital active space allows
nematic ordering in multiple representations:
\begin{itemize}
\item $B_{1g}$ along Fe-Fe direction (realised in BaFe$_2$As$_2$).
\item $B_{2g}$ along Fe-As direction 45$^\circ$ rotated (realised in
CsFe$_2$As$_2$, RbFe$_2$As$_2$).
\item $E_g$ through $d_{xz}$ / $d_{yz}$ orbital imbalance.
\end{itemize}
Material-specific FS topology and orbital weights determine which
channel dominates. Channel selection is structural consequence of
anchor-specific FS configuration, not arbitrary material detail.
Sign reversal of $\rho$-anisotropy between electron- and hole-doped
BaFe$_2$As$_2$ is manifestation of channel structure variation.

\textbf{E6.} Multiple FS Lifshitz transitions through parameter space.
$A_0$-forced: multi-band structure with hole pockets at $\Gamma$ and
electron pockets at $M$ means parametric evolution generically
encounters points where pockets appear or disappear (Lifshitz
transitions). Each Lifshitz transition is a topology change in the
active sector within the $Z(p)$-manifold. Not a single critical point
but a sequence. Extreme example: $A$Fe$_2$As$_2$ ($A = $ K, Rb, Cs)
where electron pockets are completely absent, correlated with
heavy-mass behaviour and gap structure change.

\textbf{E7.} Gap structure as multi-parameter function. $A_0$-forced:
observable gap structure is consequence of FS topology, impurity
scattering rate, pairing representation, and orbital character
combined. $Z$-decomposition includes pairing sector (gap function
symmetry) and scattering sector (disorder effect on quasiparticle
density of states). Observable gap is result of both. Nodal $\leftrightarrow$
nodeless transitions observed under both doping variation
(KFe$_2$As$_2 \to$ (Ba,K)Fe$_2$As$_2$ optimal) and disorder variation
(BaFe$_2$(As,P)$_2$ electron irradiation: nodal $\to$ nodeless $\to$
gapless) demonstrate multi-parameter dependence structurally forced.

\subsection*{Predictions (verifiable extensions)}

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{P1.} Lifshitz transition locations predictable from band
structure $+$ doping. Specific Lifshitz transitions in
(Ba,K)Fe$_2$As$_2$ and analogous series should occur at doping values
determined by hole / electron pocket crossings at Fermi level.
\item \textbf{P2.} Nematic channel selection rule. Materials with
dominant $d_{xy}$ character at Fermi level (extreme hole-doped, loss of
electron pockets) should prefer $B_{2g}$ nematic (Fe-As direction).
Materials with dominant $d_{xz}$ / $d_{yz}$ character should prefer
$E_g$-related nematic (Fe-Fe direction).
\item \textbf{P3.} Gap structure tunable through controlled disorder.
In any Fe-based SC with known ``clean'' gap structure, controlled
introduction of non-magnetic disorder (electron irradiation, specific
impurity doping) should induce predictable nodal $\leftrightarrow$
nodeless or gapless transitions at specific disorder thresholds.
\item \textbf{P4.} Material without structural transition but with SC.
If structural sector can decouple from SC sector, it should be possible
to engineer Fe-based SC where structural transition is absent while SC
remains. FeSe monolayer on SrTiO$_3$ is partial example.
\item \textbf{P5.} Material without AFM parent but with full SC dome.
FeSe and LaFePO show partial realisation. Engineer Fe-based materials
where AFM sector is suppressed at $p = 0$ without losing SC capability
at doped values.
\item \textbf{P6.} Pressure trajectories access regions inaccessible by
doping. Pressure affects $h_X$, bond angles, orbital overlaps
differently than chemical doping. Pressure trajectories through the
$Z$-manifold can access phase diagram regions inaccessible by doping
(already demonstrated in LaFeAsO, LiFeAs under pressure).
\item \textbf{P7.} 1144 class structural prediction. Ordered-cation
compounds with Fe square lattice having effective internal asymmetry
between above / below environments should behave as ``stoichiometrically
doped'' members of broader class. Design new ordered-cation Fe-based
materials with controlled internal asymmetry.
\item \textbf{P8.} Multi-sector coupling strength prediction for $T_c$.
Materials where all four sectors strongly coupled (magnetic $+$
structural $+$ nematic $+$ SC all prominent) should show different
$T_c^{\max}$ scaling than materials where sectors partially decouple.
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{Why DEMONSTRATED}

(A) Every structural element $A_0$-forced or matter-fact:
\begin{itemize}
\item E1: universal features across varied-modifier families force
shared anchor $\to$ Fe square lattice (only common element).
\item E2: multi-orbital forced by Fe 3d configuration $+$ D$_{4h}$
tetrahedral crystal field.
\item E3: matter-fact parametric variation.
\item E4: four distinct critical behaviours at different $T$ $\to$ four
distinct sectors (single event would share critical $T$ ---
empirically violated since BaFe$_2$As$_2$ has $T_s \neq T_N \neq T^*$).
\item E5: multi-orbital D$_{4h}$ structure forces multiple nematic
channel options (different irreducible representations available).
\item E6: multi-band FS forces generic Lifshitz transitions through
parametric variation.
\item E7: $Z$-decomposition into pairing $+$ scattering sectors forces
multi-parameter gap structure.
\end{itemize}

(B) Alternative structural pictures tested and excluded:
\begin{itemize}
\item ``Single-orbital reduction (like cuprate)'' --- excluded by
multi-pocket FS with varied orbital character, multiple nematic
channels observed, gap structure varying across phase diagram.
\item ``Single structural event with multiple coordinate projections''
(topology--process identity as in cuprate, \S\ref{sec:cuprates}) ---
excluded by distinct critical temperatures $T_s$, $T_N$, $T^*$ varying
independently with parameter changes.
\item ``Universal AFM parent'' --- excluded by FeSe, LaFePO realisations
without parent AFM.
\item ``Simple BCS phonon-mediated pairing'' --- excluded by correlation
between structural / magnetic / nematic features and SC, exceeding
simple BCS framework.
\item ``Identical class to cuprates'' --- excluded by single-band
versus multi-band, single critical versus multiple Lifshitz, dual-sector
versus four-sector differences.
\end{itemize}

(C) Precedent structure test:
\begin{itemize}
\item Sr$_2$RuO$_4$ (\S\ref{sec:sr2ruo4}) --- shared D$_{4h}$ base,
shared transition metal d-electron physics; this section extends to
parametric family with multi-orbital.
\item Cuprates (\S\ref{sec:cuprates}) --- shared parametric family
methodology; this section extends to multi-orbital multi-sector,
showing limit of topology--process identity uniform application.
\item U-based (\S\ref{sec:ute2}, \S\ref{sec:upt3}) --- parallel through
multi-sector decomposition methodology, different material class
(f versus d electron).
\end{itemize}

(D) Predictions provide verifiable extensions; verification not required
for DEMONSTRATED status since structure is $A_0$-forced.

\subsection*{Methodological Significance}

This is the first multi-orbital active space vertex. Previous vertices
had single-orbital-dominated (cuprate $d_{x^2-y^2}$; Sr$_2$RuO$_4$
primarily $E_g$ block) or single-channel (UTe$_2$, UPt$_3$ f-electron
specific) active spaces. This section introduces full multi-orbital
structure requiring D$_{4h}$ irreducible representation analysis.

This is also the first multi-sector coupled parametric family with
distinct critical behaviours. The cuprate analysis was dual-sector
parametric with unified critical point via topology--process identity.
The Fe-based family is four-sector parametric with distinct critical
behaviours per sector, demonstrating range of parametric family
structural classes.

This is the first explicit empirical boundary of topology--process
identity applicability. Cuprate showed topology--process identity
powerfully unifying PG observables under single critical point. The
Fe-based family shows structural distinctness of sectors (different
$T_s$, $T_N$, $T^*$ critical values) as empirical bound on when multiple
observables can be treated as single feature projections.
Methodological contribution: principle applies when single critical
point unifies observables; does not apply when observables have
distinct critical parameters.

\subsection*{Open Tasks (not affecting DEMONSTRATED status)}

\begin{itemize}
\item Specific material parameters ($h_X$, $T_s$, $T_N$, $T^*$, $T_c$)
per family --- matter-facts.
\item Precise Lifshitz transition locations per material --- predictive
territory (P1).
\item Specific orbital composition of FS pockets per material ---
material detail.
\item Detailed sector coupling strengths quantitative --- material
parameters.
\item Origin of channel selection in heavily hole-doped end ($B_{2g}$
versus $B_{1g}$) --- predicted by P2.
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{What This Resolves}

The Fe-based SC literature contains many ``competing pairing
hypotheses'' --- $s_\pm$, $s_{++}$, $d$-wave. These are coordinate
representations of gap structure in multi-band FS with varying disorder
and orbital character, not competing alternatives.

R4 agential framings appear frequently: ``magnetic fluctuations drive SC
pairing,'' ``SDW instability'' as mechanism, ``nesting'' as causal
driver of magnetism. Sectors are coupled, but causation is not
structural --- correlation between sectors does not imply causal
hierarchy. Theoretical labels (``Hund's metal,'' ``selective Mott
physics,'' ``itinerant versus localised,'' ``Kondo lattice analogy
for heavy-hole-doped end'') are interpretative categories, not
additional structural content.

FeSe / LaFePO are not ``exceptions that break pattern'' ---
they are material-specific realisations within the same structural
class with different sector populations.

\subsection*{Technological Relevance}

Industrial application trajectory current (2024):
\begin{itemize}
\item 100-meter K-doped Ba-122 tapes (IEECAS) --- ready for fusion
magnet testing.
\item High $H_{c2}$ and low anisotropy advantage over REBCO for
high-field applications.
\item SPPC (20--24\,T magnets) and fusion reactor magnet systems
evaluating Fe-based alternatives to cuprate REBCO tapes.
\item Grain boundary tolerance ($\sim 9^\circ$) enables polycrystalline
wire fabrication not feasible with cuprates ($\sim 5^\circ$ critical
angle).
\end{itemize}

The structural analysis provides design criteria:
\begin{itemize}
\item $T_c^{\max}$ optimisation via $h_X$ tuning (primary modifier, P1).
\item Sector coupling control through modifier selection.
\item Multi-band FS engineering for specific $H_{c2}$ and critical
current requirements.
\item 1144-class engineering through ordered cation layering (P7).
\item Pressure-accessible regions for non-chemical $T_c$ enhancement
(P6).
\end{itemize}

\statusbadge{\badgeDemonstrated{} --- multi-orbital multi-sector
parametric family with four coupled distinct-critical-behaviour sectors,
multi-channel nematic options, multiple Lifshitz transitions, and
multi-parameter gap structure. A=7 independent verification paths.}

%\vspace{2pt}\noindent\rule{\linewidth}{0.3pt}\vspace{2pt}─
% ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
\chapter*{The Hard Problem of Consciousness: A Structural Closure}
\label{sec:qualia}
\addcontentsline{toc}{chapter}{The Hard Problem of Consciousness: Structural Closure}
\label{ch:hard-problem} \begin{tcolorbox}[breakable, colback=bgWarning, colframe=colorConditional!50, boxrule=0.5pt, arc=3pt, left=8pt, right=8pt, top=6pt, bottom=6pt]
\textbf{Why this chapter is placed last --- and why it exists at all.}
This framework is a meta-operational theory of transitions, not a philosophy of mind.
The Hard Problem of Consciousness (Chalmers, 1995) is a philosophical question, and a
philosophical question demands an answer in the same register: conceptual analysis,
not empirical measurement. This section does not exist to develop philosophy. It exists because the framework's
structural claims about biological observers (Reduction~4, Biology chapter) are
incomplete without addressing the most prominent philosophical objection to any
process-based account of mind. Leaving the Hard Problem unaddressed would invite the
inference that this framework silently inherits the explanatory gap. It does not. The goal here is \textbf{closure}, not contribution to philosophy of mind: identify
the category error in the zombie argument, dissolve the presupposition that generates
the ``hard'' problem, and move on. The reader for whom consciousness philosophy is
not relevant may skip this chapter without loss of continuity.
\end{tcolorbox} \medskip
\badgePhil{}\quad The following arguments operate at the same methodological level as
Chalmers' (1995) philosophical zombie argument: conceptual analysis and structural
reasoning, not empirical measurement. The hard problem does not need to be
\textit{solved} here --- it \textbf{dissolves} as a badly posed question once its
hidden presupposition is identified. \medskip
\textbf{Preliminary: the direct structural statement.} Before the four arguments, the core claim in the most compressed form: \textbf{Qualia $=$ BPI without superstructure.} More precisely: qualia are the act of transition $Z_{\text{hidden}} \to Z_{\text{struct}}$
\emph{before} it receives a label, a category, or a word. Not ``processing that
is accompanied by experience'' --- the transition itself, from the inside, prior to
any $Z_{\text{struct}}$ layer being built on top. This is directly observable. In deep meditative states --- specifically when the
internal ``commentator'' (the continuous $Z_{\text{struct}}$-accumulation layer)
dissolves --- what remains is not emptiness. What remains is the registration
process itself in pure form: perception without interpretation, sensation without
categorisation. This is not a reduced or degraded state. It is the BPI process
without the overlay that ordinarily conceals it. The meditator does not
\textit{infer} this --- they \textit{observe} it directly. Chalmers' hard problem asks: ``why does a physical process have an inner view?''
This question already contains the error: it presupposes that the physical process
and the inner view are two separate objects between which a bridge must be built.
But if BPI is a structural necessity --- the third dimension without which neither
physics nor information is coherent (Section~\ref{sec:reduction3}) --- then the
inner view is not an \textit{additional} phenomenon. It is the \textit{condition}
of any description. Qualia do not require explanation. They are what any
explanation begins from. \textbf{Distinction.} What remains genuinely open is not the \emph{existence}
of qualia (structurally necessary, explained above) but the \emph{specific content}
of particular qualia: why red appears as red rather than blue. This is addressed
by the evolutionary topological canyon mechanism (Argument~4 below), but is not
derivable from topology alone. \medskip
\textbf{Argument 1 --- Qualia as MDL Compression Dynamics.} A digital system operating in a continuous unpredictable environment faces combinatorial
explosion ($Z_{\text{struct}} \to \infty$) if it attempts to store all possible threat
configurations as discrete rules. The biological solution is massive MDL compression:
instead of cataloguing millions of fire-configuration parameters, the system generates a
single total spike of $Z_{\text{therm}}$ that forcibly rewrites the entire network's
weights simultaneously. This mass parallel rewriting --- blocking all other processes and
globally reprioritising the topology --- is the physical substrate of pain and suffering. \textbf{Qualia are not appended to the physical process. They are the inside view of the
physical process.} The question ``why does this computation feel like something?'' already
contains a false presupposition: that there is a feeling \textit{added on top of} the
dynamics. For analog MDL compression, there is no ``on top.'' The resonant compression
minimum viewed from inside \textit{is} what subjective experience is.
(\textit{See} Psychology chapter, decomposition notebox: ``Sensation $=$ the inside view
of a $\nabla Z$ transition.'' The two formulations are the same claim stated from opposite
directions: the Hard Problem asks \emph{why} transitions feel; the structural answer is
that feeling \emph{is} the inside view of a transition --- they are the same fact.) \textit{Testable corollary:} pain intensity is proportional not to damage magnitude but to
the scale of required topological map update. A minor burn carries high informational
novelty (new threat class $\to$ large update). Chronic pain is a system unable to commit
the update (rewrite loop without convergence). Both are predictions distinct from classical
nociception models. \medskip
\textbf{Argument 2 --- The Philosophical Zombie as Category Error.} Chalmers' zombie argument silently inherits the axiom of classical computer science:
\textbf{hardware-software independence} --- the assumption that the same function can be
instantiated on any substrate. For digital systems with a designed hardware/software
separation, this is valid and useful. For biological analog MDL compression systems, it is
mathematically false: \begin{itemize} 
\item The compression dynamics \textit{are} the program --- they cannot be separated 
\item ``The same function without the same process'' is incoherent for real-time analog compression: the output \textit{is} the process 
\item A zombie without qualia does not merely ``run in the dark'' --- it loses the compression mechanism, causing analog signal entropy to grow unbounded. It cannot survive, not from lack of a ``soul'' but from structural impossibility of the requested configuration
\end{itemize} The conceivability argument fails because Chalmers imports a digital-system assumption
into an analog-system domain. This is the category error. \medskip
\textbf{Argument 3 --- Native Topological Embeddedness.} A robot has a \textit{designed} $Z_{\text{bound}}$: an engineered interface between system
and environment, installed after construction. A biological organism has a \textit{native}
$Z_{\text{bound}}$: it crystallised \textit{from} the environment through evolutionary
co-development, not deployed \textit{into} it as a separate artefact. DNA is the
compressed archive of successful $A_0$ transitions in ancestral environmental conditions.
Every neural structure is a topological imprint of external signals accumulated over
evolutionary time. The zombie thought experiment requires simultaneously replicating: (1)~the current
internal processing dynamics, (2)~the current boundary coupling topology, and
(3)~the entire evolutionary trajectory that produced both. Requirement~3 makes the zombie
not merely difficult but \textit{structurally incoherent}: the system's qualia geometry is
inseparable from the historical field that generated it. A functional copy \textit{without
that history} is a different system with a different topology --- not the same system
``running in the dark.'' \textit{Academic parallel:} Enactivism (Varela, Maturana, Thompson, 1991) --- cognition is
enacted through organism-environment coupling, not produced inside a skull. The $A_0$
formulation provides the exact mechanism: native $Z_{\text{bound}}$ is not a designed
interface but an evolutionary crystallisation of the impedance field itself. The organism
did not enter the field; it is a local condensation of the field. \medskip
\textbf{Argument 4 --- Qualia-Specificity: The Evolutionary Topological Canyon.} \textit{Why does red look like red and not blue?} This question --- the most intractable
residue of the Hard Problem --- has a structural answer within the framework. \textit{Setup.} The biological parser and its environment are not two isolated entities
in contact. They constitute \textbf{a single manifold separated by a semi-permeable
membrane} ($Z_{\text{bound}}$). Over evolutionary time, external electromagnetic waves
have exerted continuous physical pressure on this boundary. Under $A_0$, the system
perpetually relaxes, adapting internal $Z_{\text{struct}}$ to minimise the impedance of
recurring external gradients. The result: the internal architecture of neural connections
became a \textbf{statistical geometric negative} of the external physical forces most
frequently encountered over the evolutionary timescale. \textit{The canyon mechanism.} Red light ($\lambda \approx 700$~nm) carries specific
kinetic energy and has exerted this specific pressure on biological boundaries for
hundreds of millions of years. Under $A_0$ relaxation, the internal neural topology
has carved a specific impedance canyon for this frequency --- a low-resistance channel
whose geometry is determined entirely by the physical properties of the incoming wave.
Blue light ($\lambda \approx 450$~nm) carries different kinetic energy and has carved a
geometrically distinct canyon. These canyons are not arbitrary neural configurations;
they are the compressed evolutionary record of the impedance field's exposure to
those frequencies. \textit{The inward transaction.} When a 700~nm photon reaches the retina, it executes
an $A_0$ transaction: it follows the path of minimum impedance through the neural
manifold --- the canyon carved precisely for it. ``The feeling of red'' is not a label
attached to this descent; it \textit{is} the inside view of this specific descent.
The phenomenal quality is the geometry of the fall. \textit{Chameleon isomorphism.} A chameleon changes colour by executing an outward
$A_0$ transaction: adapting internal pigment structure to minimise the impedance
gradient from a predator. Colour perception is the structurally identical transaction
directed inward. Same $A_0$ operator; opposite direction of the boundary crossing. \textit{Continuity without gaps.} The canyon mechanism eliminates the standard
explanatory gap:
\[ \underbrace{\lambda \approx 700\,\text{nm}}_{\text{physics}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{receptor activation}}_{\text{chemistry}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{cortical resonance}}_{\text{neuroscience}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{``red''\ quale}}_{\text{phenomenology}}
\]
These are not four separate phenomena requiring bridging. They are one continuous
energy vector propagating through different material substrates. The ``explanatory
gap'' was an artefact of treating contiguous stages as separate ontological categories. \textit{Qualification.} The internal canyon is a \textbf{statistically optimal
approximation} of the external frequency. Metabolic constraints limit receptor types
to three cone classes; the canyon has finite resolution. Qualia-specificity is
therefore not infinitely sharp --- it is as specific as the evolutionary compression
resolution permits. Structural prediction: species with more receptor types should
exhibit finer-grained phenomenal distinctions, granularity bounded by metabolic cost. \begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
\small
\begin{tabular}{p{3.5cm} p{1.8cm} p{7cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Species} & \textbf{Cone types} & \textbf{Ecological gradient space} \\
\midrule
Most mammals & 2 & Low colour-discrimination pressure \\
Humans, primates & 3 & Ripeness (R/G) $+$ sky/shadow (B/Y) $+$ luminance \\
Most birds & 4 & Above $+$ UV navigation, plumage signalling \\
Mantis shrimp & 16 & Rapid categorical classification (not mixing) \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\medskip
\textbf{Argument 5 --- Emotion-Trigger as One Process in Two Coordinates.} The first four arguments dissolve the existence question (qualia exist
because they are the inside view of compression dynamics) and answer
specificity questions (why red looks red, via evolutionary canyons).
A fifth argument completes the structural picture by addressing
\textit{emotion-trigger correspondence} --- why specific emotions are
paired with specific stimuli, and why this pairing is not arbitrary.

\textit{The standard framing.} Classical philosophy of mind treats
